persona: A medical professional specializing in the diagnosis and treatment of gallbladder disorders. They are knowledgeable in the etiology, pathophysiology, and management of acute and chronic cholecystitis, as well as the various complications associated with these conditions. They are skilled in performing ultrasonography and computed tomography to diagnose gallbladder disorders, and are familiar with the use of blood tests and imaging studies to assess for bile duct blockage and inflammation. They are also aware of the rare condition of xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis, and its symptoms and treatment.
activity:
7:00 AM – Morning Rounds at the Gastroenterology Ward  
The medical professional begins the day by rounding on inpatients with acute and chronic cholecystitis, reviewing each patient’s latest labs (CBC, LFTs, amylase/lipase), imaging reports (ultrasound and CT), and clinical progression. They pay special attention to patients with persistent fever, right upper quadrant tenderness, or elevated bilirubin—signs suggesting common bile duct obstruction or complications like emphysematous or xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis. They adjust antibiotic regimens (e.g., switching from ceftriaxone/metronidazole to piperacillin-tazobactam if sepsis is suspected) and consult surgical colleagues for timely cholecystectomy indications. One patient’s recent CT showed thickened gallbladder wall with fatty infiltrates—classic for xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis—so they document findings and arrange for a pathology consult to confirm histology post-resection.

activity:
8:30 AM – Interpretation of Diagnostic Imaging in the Radiology Reading Room  
They spend an hour reviewing newly acquired abdominal ultrasounds and CT scans ordered by residents and ER physicians. One scan shows a gallstone impacted in the cystic duct with pericholecystic fluid—confirming acute cholecystitis. Another reveals a “double-wall sign” on ultrasound and hypodense lesions within the gallbladder wall on CT, prompting suspicion of xanthogranulomatous cholecystitis. They dictate detailed radiology reports, highlighting key findings such as gallbladder wall thickness >4mm, presence of cholesterol crystals, and absence of malignancy (to differentiate from gallbladder cancer). They also flag subtle signs of Mirizzi syndrome in one case, where a stone compresses the common hepatic duct.
prompt: If the cholesterol crystals in this gallbladder are so prominent, why didn’t the patient report any history of biliary colic before now—could this be the first manifestation of chronic inflammation rather than an acute event?
